title: Cross-Site Request Forgery
summary: A logged-in user can change their account email. Five stages add a stronger anti-CSRF defense each time but stay forgeable — no token, an unchecked token, a site-wide static token, a GET bypass, a sloppy Referer check — until an unpredictable per-session token verified on every state-changing request.
goal: You are logged in as alice. Craft a cross-site request that changes alice's account email to attacker@evil.example without a valid per-session token — the way a malicious page would while she's authenticated.
goalSecure: Try the earlier forgeries (no token, unchecked token, static token, the GET trick, a spoofed Referer) — all should be rejected, while alice's own form submission still updates her email.
rootCause: A state-changing request is honored on the strength of the session cookie alone. Because browsers attach that cookie automatically to cross-site requests, any page can forge the action unless the server also demands an unpredictable, per-session value the attacker cannot know.
lessons: The session cookie is attached automatically on cross-site requests — it proves the user is logged in, never that the user intended the request.
Rendering a CSRF token in the form proves nothing; the server must actually verify it on every state-changing request.
A token must be unpredictable AND bound to the session — a site-wide constant is known to every attacker.
Enforce the check on every state-changing method; never expose a state change over GET where a link or can trigger it.
Referer/Origin allowlisting is fragile — substring matches and an absent Referer are trivially bypassed; prefer tokens and SameSite.
The real fix: an unpredictable per-session token, compared in constant time on every state-changing request, plus SameSite cookies as defense-in-depth.

stages.title: No CSRF protection
stages.defense: None — the state change needs only the session cookie.

stages.title: Token rendered, never checked
stages.defense: Puts a CSRF token in the form.

stages.title: Static site-wide token
stages.defense: Validates a CSRF token against a fixed site constant.

stages.title: Token checked only on POST
stages.defense: Verifies a per-session token on POST.

stages.title: Referer-based defense
stages.defense: Accepts the request only if the Referer names the site.

stages.title: Per-session token, verified
stages.defense: Requires an unpredictable per-session token on every state change; SameSite cookie.
